text: The surgeon clips and divides the left gastroepiploic artery at its origin, followed by isolation, clipping, and division of the right gastroepiploic vein and artery. The posterior wall of the duodenum is trimmed, and the gastro-duodenal artery is exposed. The anticipated duodenal resection line is drilled, and the duodenum is divided two centimeters from the pyloric ring using a 60mm endo-GIA stapler.

The surgeon identifies and dissects number five suprapolar lymph nodes along the proximal part of the right gastric artery. The gastroduodenal artery and right gastric artery are exposed, clipped, and divided at their origins. Number twelve A lymph nodes along the proper hepatic artery are visible, and the left aberrant hepatic artery is identified. Number eight A lymph nodes are dissected along the common hepatic artery, with the common hepatic artery and portal vein clearly visible. The surgeon prepares to move to the left side to dissect number nine and number seven lymph nodes.

The surgeon identifies and dissects number seven lymph nodes along the trunk of the left gastric artery and number nine lymph nodes near the celiac artery. The left gastric artery and accompanying left gastric vein are visible. Number eleven P lymph nodes are located along the proximal splenic artery from its origin to halfway between its origin and the pancreas tail end. The surgeon proceeds to the distal splenic artery area, taking care to avoid injury to the splenic artery during dissection.

The surgeon performs dissection of number one right paracardial lymph nodes and number three lesser capsule lymph nodes along the branches of the left gastric artery. The vagus nerve is encountered and divided using a harmonic scalpel. The lesser curvature area is trimmed, and the stomach is divided five centimeters proximal to the upper margin of the tumor. The specimen is placed in a bag and extracted through the umbilical wound.

The video shows the pericardial lymph nodes after the stomach resection. The number four SB area, at the origin of the left gastroepiploic artery, is visible. Moving right, the ciliac axis is seen with number nine, number seven, number eleven P, and number eight A lymph nodes. Further to the right, the stumps of the right gastric artery and right gastroepiploic artery, corresponding to the number six area, are visible, along with the duodenal stump. This is part of the DII lymph node dissection.
